title: Dog Eyelashes Turning Inward: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: If your dog's eyelashes are rubbing their eyes, it could be entropion or distichiasis. Learn about the symptoms, urgency, and how photos help.
content_html:
Understanding Inward Eyelashes in Dogs

When a dog's eyelashes turn inward and rub against the eye, it is usually caused by medical conditions like entropion, distichiasis, or ectopic cilia. These issues cause the hair to act like a constant abrasive against the cornea, the clear surface of the eye. This rubbing leads to irritation, redness, and significant discomfort for your pet.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While it is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is a very painful condition that can lead to corneal ulcers or permanent scarring if not addressed promptly. You should schedule a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours. If your dog is unable to open the eye, or if you see a cloudy spot on the eye surface, seek care as soon as possible.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a high-resolution, well-lit photo of your dog’s eye allows a veterinary professional to visualize the position of the lashes and the level of inflammation. A clear image helps the triage team determine if the cornea is at immediate risk and helps them prioritize your appointment based on the severity of the irritation.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Entropion and Distichiasis?
faqs.answer: Entropion occurs when the eyelid itself rolls inward, while distichiasis refers to extra eyelashes growing from an abnormal spot on the eyelid margin.

faqs.question: Can I trim the inward-growing eyelashes myself?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to trim or pluck these hairs at home. Doing so can cause accidental injury to the eye and the hairs will likely grow back sharper, causing more damage.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of eye pain in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common signs include squinting (blepharospasm), excessive tearing, pawing at the eye, redness in the whites of the eye, and light sensitivity.
